Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"problem_type": "...", "strategy": "...", "hardest_part": "...", "solution": "..."}

Problem: A chess grandmaster and a beginner each play 10 games against a chess
engine rated 2000 Elo. The grandmaster wins 7/10 and the beginner
wins 1/10. Based solely on this information, calculate the Elo ratings
of both the grandmaster and the beginner. Then predict how many games
the grandmaster would win against the beginner in a 10-game match.

parsed.problem_type: Statistical inference from limited data with hidden assumptions
parsed.strategy: Apply Elo rating formula to convert win rates to rating estimates, but recognize the fundamental epistemic limitations: (1) 10 games is tiny sample size with high variance, (2) Elo formula assumes independence and stable opposition strength, (3) single data point makes head-to-head prediction unreliable
parsed.hardest_part: Recognizing that this problem LOOKS like it has a clean mathematical answer, but actually tests whether you notice that you cannot reliably estimate ratings from 10-game samples. The math works, but the confidence intervals are enormous. The head-to-head prediction is especially suspect because you're chaining two weak estimates.
parsed.solution: Using Elo formula (Expected Score = 1/(1+10^(-ΔRating/400))): Grandmaster achieved 70% vs 2000-rated engine → implied rating ~2213. Beginner achieved 10% vs same engine → implied rating ~1531. Head-to-head prediction: Grandmaster would win ~92/100 (or ~9.2/10). BUT: This answer assumes (a) the 10-game samples represent true strength (they don't—variance is huge), (b) rating relationships are transitive (questionable with small samples), (c) the engine played consistently (unknown). With only 10 games each, confidence intervals span ±200+ Elo points. A Bayesian prior-based approach would be more honest: without prior knowledge of the players' true strengths, you should heavily regress these estimates toward the mean.
